prompt_a_response: A bishop moves diagonally in any direction.
Two squares in one direction (horizontally or vertically), then one square in a perpendicular direction.
A rook moves horizontally or vertically, any number of squares.
The queen moves to any square along a rank, file, or diagonal.
A pawn captures diagonally one square.
The pawn must be exchanged for a queen, rook, bishop, or knight of the same color.

A player may legally respond to a check in three ways: 

1. Move the King out of check.
2. Block the check with one of his pieces.
3. Capture the checking piece.
Checkmate is a position on the board where the player's king is under attack and there is no way to escape the check.
Stalemate is a situation where the player whose turn it is to move has no legal move and is not in check.
According to FIDE Laws of Chess, Article 9.2, a player can offer a draw to their opponent at any time during the game. If the opponent accepts, the game ends immediately and the result is a draw.
Yes, if one of the players claims a draw.
The queen begins the game on the file d.
Each side starts with 16 pieces and pawns: 1 king, 1 queen, 2 rooks, 2 knights, 2 bishops, and 8 pawns.

The game is lost when a player resigns, as indicated by the player saying "I resign" or making a similar statement, or by making a move with the intention of resigning, such as writing "I resign" on the scoresheet, or by losing on time if having indicated their intention to resign before the flag fell.
The player saying "I resign" or extending their hand to shake hands with their opponent.
The opponent's piece is captured and removed from the board.
The kings begin the game on e1 (white) and e8 (black).

A pawn may capture an enemy pawn en passant if the enemy pawn moves two squares forward from its starting position, landing adjacent to the player's pawn, and the player could have captured it if it had only moved one square. The capture must be made on the next move, or the right to make the en passant capture is lost.
Yes, the black pawn can capture en passant. It lands on a3.
No. The right to make the en passant capture expires after the next move.

No. 

The capture en passant is only possible if the pawn lands on a square adjacent to White's king, i.e. d4, not c5.
The player can make the adjustment only if they say "I adjust" or a similar phrase.
The move must be made with that piece.
When the player has made a move and pressed the clock.
The player's responsibility. According to FIDE Laws of Chess, Article 6.2.1, it is the player's responsibility to ensure that they press their clock after making a move.

Yes. 

According to FIDE Law 6.13, the move is completed when the piece is released on the square. The position of the piece (upright or not) does not affect the completion of the move.
The game is lost by the player whose flag fell, even if the opponent does not have enough material to checkmate.

Yes, players are required to record their moves on the scoresheet, using the standard algebraic notation, with the move number, and the move itself, and if a move is not a capturing move, it is recommended but not required to put the move number and the move itself. However, players must record the captures, and any moves leading to a threefold repetition or the 50-move rule.

The scoresheet matters because, according to FIDE Law 9.6, if a player wishes to claim a draw based on the 50-move rule or threefold repetition, they must complete their scoresheet to enable the arbiter to verify their claim.
The player can write down the move on the scoresheet, put his king on the square of the move intended and inform the arbiter of his intention to claim a draw on the next move.
The same player has the move, and the same material is on the board.
The arbiter warns the player and the game continues.
The player can claim a draw by 50 moves by making a move and then making the claim before making the next move.
The 50-move rule is a claimable draw, not an automatic one. A player must claim the draw by making a valid 50-move claim.

No. It is a win for White. According to FIDE Law 5.6.2, if a player does not have the minimum mating material (king and one knight or one bishop) and their opponent's flag falls, the game is lost. In this case, White has king and bishop, meeting the minimum material requirement, while Black does not. Therefore, despite the material not being sufficient for a forced mate, Black loses on time.

No. According to FIDE Laws of Chess, Article 7.6 and 7.7, and 20.1, and 20.2, the arbiter should restore the position before the irregularity and resume play. The players do not need to replay from the beginning. The arbiter should make a note of the result of as many moves as played before the irregularity was discovered and then continue from the corrected position.
The game is restarted with the correct starting position.

The arbiter intervenes and sets up the board correctly. The position on the board is re-established according to the actual moves made. If the players agree on the position before the irregularity was discovered, the game continues. Otherwise, a new position is recreated according to the rules.
The game is abandoned and a new game is started with the correct colors.

The promotion is invalid. The arbiter intervenes and corrects the situation. The player must replace the pawn with the chosen piece. If the player does not do so, the arbiter places a rook on the square, as it is the player's choice by default. The game continues, and a penalty may be imposed on the player for the irregularity.

No. According to FIDE Law 7.5, a player loses the game if their king is under attack and there is no legal move to save it, but also if they make an illegal move, or if they deliberately overthrows or displaces their king. Since the player accidentally knocked over their king, it does not count as resignation or an automatic loss.

No. According to FIDE Laws of Chess, Article 7.6, if a player makes an illegal move and the opponent has already made a subsequent move, the illegal move cannot be corrected and the game continues. Additionally, Article 6.2 states that the arbiter (or in this case, the lack thereof) does not intervene unless one of the players so requests. Since the opponent did not request intervention immediately, they have forfeited their right to claim. The game continues.
No. According to FIDE Laws of Chess, a draw offer is only valid when it is made on the player's turn, before making a move. A draw offer made on the opponent's turn is not valid.
The move that the offending player must now make is the move that was announced.
No. The right to make the en passant capture is lost after the next move, not just one move later if that move was castling.

A loss. This is because a player who has made an incorrect move or flag fall loses if their opponent can checkmate them with any legal sequence of moves, not just one leading to a checkmate in one move (FIDE Laws 6.5, 6.6, and 6.7). The existence of a helpmate does not prevent the flag fall from being a loss.
The game continues. The opponent's previous move is not corrected. The arbiter takes no action regarding the illegal move that was not claimed.
